schools.summary: Paul reminds the Corinthians that all the Israelites ('our fathers') enjoyed great privileges — all were under the cloud (the pillar of cloud, the symbol of God's guiding presence, Exodus 13:21) and all passed through the Red Sea on dry ground, signal tokens of divine protection and favour shared by the whole nation (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Henry): Paul recounts these high privileges to warn the Corinthians — for though all Israel shared them, most perished; great privileges are no guarantee of safety.

themes: all our fathers were under the cloud
all passed through the sea
the privileges of the fathers
great privileges no guarantee of safety

schools.summary: Paul represents the Israelites as 'baptized unto Moses' — by passing under the cloud and through the sea they were initiated into the covenant under Moses's leadership, the cloud above and the sea around them the media of this symbolic baptism, a figure analogous to Christian baptism into Christ (Barnes, Robertson).
Wesley: as the Israelites were baptized unto Moses, so Christians are baptized unto Christ; the privilege corresponds, and so does the warning that follows.

themes: were all baptized unto Moses in the cloud and in the sea
the fathers baptized unto Moses
Israel's initiation under Moses
the parallel with Christian baptism

schools.summary: The 'spiritual meat' is the manna (Exodus 16) — called 'spiritual' not because of any peculiar nature but because it was supernaturally given, a token of divine favour, and a type pointing to Christ; all the Israelites alike ate this miraculous food, a common privilege of the whole nation (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Wesley): the manna was a figure of Christ, the true bread from heaven (John 6:31-33); the fathers fed, in their measure, on the same Christ believers now feed on.

themes: did all eat the same spiritual meat
the fathers eating spiritual meat
the manna typifying Christ
the typical feeding of Israel

schools.summary: The 'spiritual drink' is the water miraculously given from the rock (Exodus 17:6; Numbers 20:11); 'the Rock that followed them' means the stream of water that accompanied Israel, not literally the rock moving (though Paul may adapt a rabbinic legend of the following rock). Robertson takes 'the Rock was Christ' as stating in symbolic form the pre-existence of Christ — the source of the water; Barnes reads 'was' as 'represented,' the rock a vivid emblem of Christ, not the pre-incarnate Christ literally following.

Calvin: the rock was a sacramental sign, Christ connected with it not locally or substantially but sacramentally; the fathers truly partook of Christ's flesh and blood by the secret work of the Spirit, though his flesh did not yet exist — Christ now presented more fully according to the measure of revelation.

Cyril (with Clarke, Ellicott): the Rock was the pre-existent Christ himself, whom Moses saw — God manifest in the Rock under the old dispensation as he was manifest in the flesh under the new; the abiding presence that sustained Israel.

debates.question: Does 'that Rock was Christ' assert the pre-existent Christ himself present with Israel, or that the rock represented/typified Christ?

sides.position: The pre-existent Christ himself — the spiritual Rock, source of the water and Israel's abiding presence (his pre-existence and deity)
'Was' means 'represented' — the rock a vivid emblem of Christ, not the pre-incarnate Christ literally following

themes: they drank of that spiritual Rock that followed them
that Rock was Christ
the spiritual drink from the Rock
the pre-existence of Christ crux

schools.summary: The sharp contrast ('but'): notwithstanding all their privileges (vv.1-4), God was not pleased with the greater part of them, their sin provoking his displeasure; 'overthrown' (strewn down, scattered as corpses) — the vast majority fell in the wilderness and never entered Canaan (Numbers 14; Hebrews 3:17, Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Wesley): privileges and sacraments did not save the unfaithful; the Corinthians, trusting their own privileges, must take warning from Israel's overthrow.

themes: with many of them God was not well pleased
they were overthrown in the wilderness
most of the fathers perished
privileges did not preserve the unfaithful

schools.summary: 'Examples' (typoi, types, patterns) — Israel's history was written as a warning pattern for Christians; their experiences typify the dangers and judgments believers must avoid; the first lesson being not to lust (crave evil things) as the Israelites did, who craved the fleshpots of Egypt and despised God's provision (Numbers 11, Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Henry): God recorded Israel's sins and judgments precisely to admonish later generations; the Corinthians, and we, are to read them as warnings against the same evil desires.

themes: these things were our examples
we should not lust after evil things, as they also lusted
Israel's fall a warning example
the typical function of the wilderness narrative

schools.summary: The second sin to be shunned: idolatry — as some of the Israelites became idolaters, worshipping the golden calf (Exodus 32); Paul quotes Exodus 32:6, 'the people sat down to eat and drink, and rose up to play,' the feasting and licentious revelry that accompanied the calf-worship, a warning bearing directly on the Corinthian danger of idol-feasts (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Wesley): the idol-feasts at Corinth, with their eating and revelry, were of a piece with Israel's idolatry; Paul warns the Corinthians not to repeat it.

themes: Neither be ye idolaters, as were some of them
The people sat down to eat and drink, and rose up to play
the warning against idolatry
the chief peril at Corinth

schools.summary: The third sin: fornication — as some Israelites did at Baal-peor, joining idolatry to immorality with the daughters of Moab (Numbers 25); 'fell in one day three and twenty thousand' — Numbers 25:9 gives twenty-four thousand, reconciled by taking Paul's figure as those who fell in the single day of the plague (the rest dying by the judges or on following days), or as a rounding (Barnes, Lightfoot).
Calvin: the swift and terrible judgment on Israel's fornication warns the Corinthians against the immorality bound up with their idolatry.

themes: Neither let us commit fornication, as some of them committed
fell in one day three and twenty thousand
the warning against fornication
the link of idolatry and immorality

schools.summary: The fourth sin: tempting Christ (provoking, putting to the test) — as the Israelites tempted God in the wilderness (Numbers 21:5-6); the reading 'Christ' (in many manuscripts) implies that Christ was present with Israel and was the one they tempted, a testimony to his pre-existence (cf. v.4); and they were destroyed by the fiery serpents (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Wesley): to tempt Christ is to presume upon his patience and provoke his judgment; the Corinthians, by their license, were in danger of doing the same.

themes: Neither let us tempt Christ, as some of them also tempted
were destroyed of serpents
the warning against tempting Christ
Christ present with Israel

schools.summary: The fifth and last sin: murmuring (complaining, discontent against God and his appointed leaders) — as the Israelites murmured (Numbers 14; 16) against Moses and against God's dealings; 'the destroyer' being the destroying angel or agent of God's judgment who smote the murmurers (Numbers 16:41-49, Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Wesley): murmuring against God's providence and his ministers brought destruction on Israel; the Corinthians, murmuring against Paul and chafing at restraint, are warned.

themes: Neither murmur ye, as some of them also murmured
were destroyed of the destroyer
the warning against murmuring
discontent against God

schools.summary: These events befell Israel 'for ensamples' (typoi, types) — designed as patterns and warnings, not mere history — recorded in Scripture for our admonition (instruction, warning); and Christians live in 'the ends of the ages,' the last dispensation, the gospel age toward which all former ages pointed, so the warnings press with peculiar force (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: they were written for our admonition, Christians being the intended beneficiaries of these recorded judgments — the wilderness history a standing warning to the church.

themes: all these things happened unto them for ensamples
written for our admonition, upon whom the ends of the world are come
Israel's history written for us
the warnings of the last age

schools.summary: The practical conclusion: the one who supposes himself secure (firm in faith, beyond danger) is precisely the one in peril; confidence in one's own security is no evidence that we are safe, and may be one of the strongest evidences that we are in danger; all professed Christians are to take heed (Barnes, Robertson, with Clarke noting 'thinketh' strengthens rather than weakens the sense — he who most confidently stands).

Calvin: this reproves not the assurance of faith (which rests on God's promises and is inseparable from faith) but the false carnal security of the flesh — the puffed-up self-confidence by which men give themselves no concern; against the Papists, Paul does not teach that faith must doubt, nor that the elect finally fall; the elect are humbled and so kept.

Clarke: let him who most confidently stands take heed lest he fall — not only partially but finally; in a state of probation everything may change, and the highest saint can stand no longer than he depends on God and continues in the obedience of faith.

debates.question: Does 'take heed lest he fall' teach that a true believer may finally fall away?

sides.position: A real possibility of final apostasy — the highest saint may fall, not only partially but finally, in a state of probation
A warning against carnal self-confidence, not the assurance of faith; the elect are humbled and so kept, not finally falling

themes: let him that thinketh he standeth take heed lest he fall
the warning against self-confidence
self-distrust
the perseverance crux

schools.summary: Paul reassures: the temptations (trials, tests) they face are only 'common to man' — such as all men experience, not beyond human endurance; the ground of confidence being God's faithfulness, who will not permit them to be tempted beyond their strength, but with every temptation provides a 'way of escape' (a way out), that they may be able to bear it — not always removal, but strength and a path through (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Chrysostom): God measures the trial to the strength he gives and faithfully makes a way of escape; the believer's confidence rests not on his own strength but on God's fidelity.

themes: no temptation taken you but such as is common to man
God is faithful, who will not suffer you to be tempted above that ye are able
God's faithfulness in temptation
the way of escape

schools.summary: Paul draws the practical conclusion from all the foregoing (the wilderness warnings, the faithfulness of God): 'flee from idolatry' — not merely avoid it, but flee (run from, shun utterly) every form of it, including the idol-feasts; the affectionate address ('my dearly beloved') showing the warning flows from love (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: idolatry is so deadly that the only safe course is flight; one is not to dally with it or test how near one may come, but to flee.

themes: my dearly beloved, flee from idolatry
the command to flee idolatry
idolatry to be fled, not flirted with
the warning flowing from love

schools.summary: Paul addresses the Corinthians as 'wise men' — those able to weigh an argument — and invites them to 'judge' (discern, evaluate) for themselves what he is about to say about the Lord's Supper and idol-feasts; appealing to their own reason and sense, confident that if they weigh the matter fairly they will see the truth (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Henry): the argument he is about to make (vv.16-22) is so clear that their own wisdom, fairly exercised, will confirm it; he does not dictate but reasons.

themes: I speak as to wise men; judge ye what I say
Paul appealing to their judgment
reasoning with their discernment
the respectful, persuasive method

schools.summary: 'The cup of blessing' is the eucharistic cup, so called because over it God is blessed and praised for redemption (the Jewish 'cup of blessing'); 'which we bless' means set apart by thanksgiving to a sacred use, no physical change implied. The 'communion' (koinonia) is the participation by which believers share alike in the benefits of Christ's blood and body and express their union with him and one another — the cup and bread emblems exhibiting this; Robertson: a spiritual participation in the blood of Christ symbolized by the cup (Barnes, Robertson).

Calvin: the cup is figuratively the communion of the blood, but the figure is not empty — the reality is present, and the soul truly partakes of Christ's blood as the mouth drinks the wine; believers are united to Christ so as to be flesh of his flesh, a real spiritual participation against both an empty memorial and the Papist transubstantiation and withholding of the cup.

debates.question: Is the 'communion of the blood of Christ' a real spiritual participation in Christ or an emblem of shared benefit and union?

sides.position: A real spiritual participation — the soul truly partakes of Christ's blood as the mouth drinks the wine (spiritual real presence)
The participation by which believers share alike in the benefits of Christ's blood and express union with him and each other, the cup and bread emblems

themes: The cup of blessing which we bless, is it not the communion of the blood of Christ
The bread which we break, is it not the communion of the body of Christ
the communion in the Supper
the eucharistic crux

schools.summary: Paul draws from the Supper the unity of the church: though believers are many, they are 'one bread' (one loaf) and 'one body,' united into a single body by their common partaking; the shared participation in the one loaf both the sign and seal of their union, all eating of the one bread and so made one body in Christ (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Wesley): the Supper binds believers into one body in Christ; therefore to partake also at the table of idols would be to rend that union and join oneself to idolaters.

themes: we being many are one bread, and one body
we are all partakers of that one bread
the church's unity in the one bread
the union effected in the Supper

schools.summary: Paul points to 'Israel after the flesh' (the natural Israel, the Jewish nation in their temple worship) as a parallel: those who ate of the Jewish sacrifices were thereby 'partakers of the altar' — joined in fellowship with the altar and the God worshipped there; eating the sacrifice signified communion in the worship (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: as eating the temple sacrifice united the Israelite to the altar and its God, so eating the idol-sacrifice would unite the Corinthian to the idol's altar — the very thing to be shunned.

themes: Behold Israel after the flesh
are not they which eat of the sacrifices partakers of the altar
the argument from Israel's altar
eating the sacrifice as communion

schools.summary: Paul anticipates an objection: does he mean to say that the idol is really something (a true god), or that the meat offered to it is changed or has any real power? He had already taught (8:4) that an idol is nothing; not retracting that, he raises the question to prevent the inference that, by warning against idol-feasts, he attributes reality to idols (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: the idol in itself is nothing, yet (as the next verse shows) there is a real spiritual danger behind it; Paul holds both truths together.

themes: that the idol is any thing, or that which is offered in sacrifice to idols is any thing
guarding against misunderstanding about the idol
the idol nothing in itself
yet a real danger behind it

schools.summary: Though the idol is nothing, the pagan sacrifices are in fact offered 'to devils' (daimonia, demons) — behind the lifeless idol stand real evil spirits, who are the true objects and beneficiaries of idol-worship; these sacrifices, whatever the worshippers suppose, are not offered to the true God but to demons, a real and dark spiritual transaction (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: to partake of the idol-feast is to enter into fellowship (communion) with demons; this is the real peril behind the seemingly indifferent meat, which Paul would have them utterly shun.

themes: the things which the Gentiles sacrifice, they sacrifice to devils, and not to God
I would not that ye should have fellowship with devils
pagan sacrifices offered to devils
the demonic reality behind idolatry

schools.summary: Paul declares the two utterly incompatible: one cannot partake of the cup of the Lord (in the Supper, communion with Christ) and the cup of devils (in the idol-feast, fellowship with demons); the Lord's table signifies communion with Christ and the table of devils communion with demons, so to share both is impossible — one cannot belong to Christ and to demons at once (Barnes, Robertson).
Cyril (with Wesley): the believer must choose — partaking of the Lord's table binds him to Christ alone, forbidding any communion with the table of devils.

themes: Ye cannot drink the cup of the Lord, and the cup of devils
ye cannot be partakers of the Lord's table, and of the table of devils
the incompatibility of the two tables
exclusive allegiance to Christ

schools.summary: Paul presses the point with two pointed questions: by sharing the idol-feast, 'do we provoke the Lord to jealousy?' — stirring up his righteous jealousy, as Israel did (Deuteronomy 32:21), by giving to idols the devotion due to him alone; and 'are we stronger than he?' — exposing the folly of provoking God while utterly unable to resist his judgment (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: God is a jealous God who will not share his worship with idols; to provoke him is to court certain ruin, for none can stand against him.

themes: Do we provoke the Lord to jealousy? are we stronger than he
the warning against provoking the Lord's jealousy
God's righteous jealousy
the folly of resisting God

schools.summary: Paul takes up the Corinthian maxim 'all things are lawful' (perhaps their slogan) and qualifies it: even of things truly indifferent and lawful, not all are 'expedient' (profitable, helpful), and not all 'edify' (build up the church, benefit others); the believer's liberty must be governed by what builds up, not merely by what is permitted (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Henry): Christian liberty is real but not absolute; it is to be limited by the good of others and the edification of the church, not used selfishly.

themes: All things are lawful for me, but all things are not expedient
all things edify not
liberty limited by expediency and edification
the qualification of Christian liberty

schools.summary: The governing rule of Christian liberty: let no one seek merely his own (his own interest, advantage, gratification), but each seek 'another's wealth' (the other's good, welfare, benefit) — the law of unselfish love; 'wealth' here meaning well-being and profit, not riches (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Wesley): this is the heart of the whole matter of liberty and idol-meats — self is to yield to the good of the brother; love seeks the neighbour's edification above its own rights.

themes: Let no man seek his own, but every man another's wealth
the rule of seeking another's good
unselfish love
liberty governed by the neighbour's good

schools.summary: Paul grants real liberty: whatever meat is sold in the 'shambles' (the public meat-market, where much had been offered to idols) may be eaten freely, the believer eating 'asking no question' — not making anxious inquiry whether it had been offered to an idol; to do so would needlessly burden the conscience, since the meat itself is indifferent (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Chrysostom): in itself the meat is clean, and the believer's liberty is real; the scruple arises only when conscience is involved (v.28).

themes: Whatsoever is sold in the shambles, that eat
asking no question for conscience sake
freedom to eat market meat
liberty without scruple

schools.summary: Paul grounds the freedom to eat in Psalm 24:1: 'the earth is the Lord's, and the fulness thereof' — all the produce of the earth belongs to God, who gives it for human use; so the meat is clean and good in itself, an idol's claim unable to defile what belongs to the Lord and is received with thanksgiving (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: God's ownership sanctifies the use of his creatures; the believer may freely enjoy what God has made and given, the idol having no real power to pollute it.

themes: the earth is the Lord's, and the fulness thereof
liberty grounded in God's ownership
God's gift making all clean
the divine ownership freeing the conscience

schools.summary: Paul addresses the social case: if an unbeliever invites a Christian to a meal, and he is willing to go (Paul does not forbid such friendly social intercourse), he may attend and eat whatever is served without anxious inquiry about its origin — the same rule as for the market (v.25), the meat being indifferent (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Cyril): the liberty holds so long as no question of conscience is raised; the next verse provides the exception when someone points out the meat as idol-offered.

themes: If any of them that believe not bid you to a feast
whatsoever is set before you, eat, asking no question for conscience sake
liberty at an unbeliever's table
freedom in social life

schools.summary: The exception to the liberty: if someone present (perhaps a weak brother, or a scrupulous heathen) expressly points out that the meat had been offered to an idol, the believer is to abstain — not because the meat is in itself defiling, but 'for his sake' (the one who pointed it out) and for conscience' sake, lest he wound the weak conscience or seem to countenance idolatry (Barnes, Robertson).
Wesley: love yields its liberty when its exercise would harm another's conscience; the abstention is not from any defilement in the meat but out of regard for the other.

themes: if any man say, This is offered in sacrifice unto idols, eat not
for his sake that shewed it, and for conscience sake
abstain when the meat is pointed out
liberty yielded for another's conscience

schools.summary: Paul clarifies which conscience he means: not the eating believer's own conscience (which is free, knowing the meat indifferent) but the conscience of 'the other' (the weak brother or observer) — it is his scruple, not the eater's, that calls for abstention; Paul anticipating the objection, 'why should my real liberty be judged by another's conscience?' (Barnes, Robertson).

Calvin: the believer's own conscience remains free; he yields his liberty voluntarily for love's sake, not because the other's scruple binds him in truth — the answer to the objection being the law of love and the glory of God.

themes: Conscience, I say, not thine own, but of the other
why is my liberty judged of another man's conscience
the other's conscience limiting liberty
liberty yielded, not bound

schools.summary: If Paul partakes of food 'by grace' (with thankfulness, as God's gift, or by God's favour), why should he be evil spoken of (blamed, reproached) for eating that for which he gives thanks? The food received with thanksgiving is sanctified to the believer (1 Timothy 4:4-5); his thankful enjoyment of God's gift should not expose him to reproach (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: this confirms the believer's real liberty — yet, in harmony with vv.28-29, he will still forgo it where its use would give offence; thankfulness sanctifies the gift, but love governs its use.

themes: if I by grace be a partaker, why am I evil spoken of for that for which I give thanks
the thankful enjoyment of God's gifts
the gift sanctified by thanksgiving
liberty real yet governed by love

schools.summary: Paul gathers the whole discussion of meats and liberty into one grand principle: even in the most ordinary acts — eating and drinking — and indeed in 'whatsoever ye do,' the believer is to act with a view to God's glory; this the supreme and universal standard of the Christian life, every act to be done so as to honour God, the single aim that resolves all questions of liberty and conscience (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Wesley): nothing is too small to be consecrated to God's glory; this principle, rightly applied, governs the use of all liberty and sanctifies the whole of life.

themes: Whether therefore ye eat, or drink, or whatsoever ye do, do all to the glory of God
the great principle of God's glory
God's glory the aim of every act
the comprehensive standard of life

schools.summary: Paul applies the glory of God to conduct toward others: give 'none offence' (no occasion of stumbling or scandal) to anyone — naming three classes: the Jews, the Gentiles (Greeks), and the church of God; 'offence' meaning a stumbling-block, anything that would needlessly hinder another's salvation or wound the church (Barnes, Robertson).

Calvin (with Wesley): the believer's regard for others extends to all — unbelieving Jew and Gentile (lest they be repelled from the gospel) and fellow Christians (lest they stumble); love consults the good of all.

themes: Give none offence, neither to the Jews, nor to the Gentiles, nor to the church of God
the rule of giving no offence
no stumbling-block to any
universal regard for others

schools.summary: Paul offers himself as the pattern: he seeks to please (accommodate, conciliate) all men in all things — not from man-pleasing flattery, but adapting himself for their good, as he had described (9:19-22); not seeking his own profit but the profit (benefit) of the many, subordinating his own interest and liberty to others' welfare, with the supreme aim that they may be saved (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: the great end of all this self-accommodation is the salvation of others — love bends every liberty toward this highest good, that they may be saved.

themes: even as I please all men in all things, not seeking mine own profit
but the profit of many, that they may be saved
Paul's example of self-denying love
liberty bent toward others' salvation
